children.title: PART I: ORBIT
Orbital Anatomy
Bony Orbit
Volume: ~30mL adult; pear-shaped; widest 1cm behind orbital rim
7 bones: frontal, zygomatic, maxilla, palatine, lacrimal, ethmoid, sphenoid
Roof: frontal bone (lacrimal fossa anterolateral; trochlear fossa anteromedial); thin posteriorly → frontal lobe
Lateral wall: strongest; zygomatic (anterior) + greater wing of sphenoid (posterior); separates orbit from temporal fossa/middle cranial fossa
Medial wall: thinnest (lamina papyracea = ethmoid); adjacent to ethmoid sinuses; fractures easily; conducts infection from sinuses
Floor: maxilla (body) + zygomatic + palatine; thinnest at infraorbital groove; overlies maxillary sinus; blow-out fracture site
Orbital Apertures
Optic canal: lesser wing of sphenoid; CN II + ophthalmic artery; 5-6mm long
Superior orbital fissure (SOF): between greater and lesser wings of sphenoid; CN III, IV, V1 (lacrimal, frontal, nasociliary), VI, superior ophthalmic vein, sympathetic fibers
SOF mnemonic: 'Lazy French Tarts Sit Naked In Anticipation' (Lacrimal, Frontal, Trochlear, Superior div CN III, Nasociliary, Inferior div CN III, Abducens)
Inferior orbital fissure: between greater sphenoid wing and maxilla/palatine; V2 (infraorbital), inferior ophthalmic vein, zygomatic nerve
Anterior ethmoid foramen: medial wall; anterior ethmoid artery + nerve (~24mm from anterior lacrimal crest)
Posterior ethmoid foramen: ~12mm posterior to anterior; posterior ethmoid artery
Nasolacrimal canal: medial floor; carries nasolacrimal duct to inferior meatus
Orbital Soft Tissues
Periorbita: periosteum lining orbit; loosely attached except at sutures and foramina; surgical plane for subperiosteal dissection
Intraorbital ON: 25-30mm long; redundant (allows eye movement); surrounded by meninges
Annulus of Zinn: fibrous ring at orbital apex; origin of 4 recti; SOF structures inside vs outside ring
Orbital fat: extraocular (between periorbita and muscle cone) + intraconal; maintains globe position; herniation through septum = dermatochalasis
Orbital Vasculature

Ophthalmic artery: first branch of ICA; enters optic canal; branches: central retinal artery, posterior ciliary arteries, muscular branches, anterior ethmoid, posterior ethmoid, lacrimal, supraorbital, supratrochlear
Venous drainage: superior ophthalmic vein → cavernous sinus; inferior ophthalmic vein → cavernous sinus or pterygoid plexus; no valves → bidirectional flow
Orbital Nerves
CN V1 (ophthalmic): lacrimal nerve (LG + lateral lid) + frontal nerve (supraorbital + supratrochlear) + nasociliary nerve (corneal sensation, ciliary ganglion)
Ciliary ganglion: lies lateral to ON; parasympathetic (CN III) + sympathetic + sensory (nasociliary) roots; short ciliary nerves → sphincter + ciliary muscle
Lacrimal gland: secretomotor from CN VII via greater petrosal nerve → vidian nerve → pterygopalatine ganglion → maxillary nerve → zygomatic nerve → lacrimal nerve
Evaluation of Orbital Disorders
History
Pain: acute + severe → hemorrhage, infection, mucormycosis, acute angle closure; dull aching → inflammation (TED, scleritis, pseudotumor); sharp lancinating → nerve involvement
Onset speed: seconds-hours → hemorrhage, CCF; days → cellulitis, pseudotumor; weeks-months → neoplasm, TED
Proptosis: axial (intraconal) vs eccentric (extraconal, localises to direction away from lesion)
Physical Examination
Proptosis measurement: Hertel exophthalmometer; normal <20mm; asymmetry >2mm significant
Resistance to retropulsion: increased in TED, tumour; pulsatile = CCF or bony defect (NF1)
Globe position: axial (intraconal mass) vs displacement (extraconal; displaced away from lesion)
Palpation: anterior orbital rim for masses; lymph nodes; scalp for neurofibromas
Auscultation: bruit → CCF (pulsatile tinnitus + red eye + raised IOP + dilated episcleral vessels)
Imaging
CT: bone detail (fractures, erosion, calcification); metallic foreign bodies; fast; no MRI contraindication issues; axial + coronal cuts; IV contrast for vascular lesions
MRI: soft tissue, ON, brain; T1 (fat bright, anatomy), T2 (water/fluid bright, inflammation), fat-suppressed Gad (ON enhancement, active inflammation)
CT preferred: trauma, bony lesions, thyroid eye disease (EOM measurements), acute setting, pre-op decompression planning
MRI preferred: ON/brain involvement, optic nerve sheath meningioma, soft tissue characterisation, cavernous sinus
Ultrasound: A + B scan; acoustic characteristics of lesions; cavernous hemangioma (high internal reflectivity); compressibility; ONSD >5mm → raised ICP
Angiography/MRA/CTA: CCF, AVM, vascular malformations; preoperative planning for highly vascular tumors
Biopsy: anterior orbitotomy for accessible lesions; fine needle aspiration (FNA) for lymphoma, metastasis; incisional biopsy if FNA insufficient; excisional for small encapsulated lesions
Congenital Orbital Anomalies
Anophthalmia: absent globe; stimulate socket growth with progressively sized conformers from birth; prevents orbital hypoplasia; dermis fat graft or implant later
Microphthalmia: small globe; conformers/expanders for socket development; associated CHARGE syndrome, coloboma, chromosomal anomalies
Craniofacial Syndromes
Crouzon: bilateral coronal synostosis; hypertelorism; proptosis (shallow orbits); exorbitism; midface hypoplasia; FGFR2; no syndactyly (unlike Apert)
Apert: coronal synostosis + syndactyly; exorbitism; V-shaped palate; FGFR2
Treatment: multidisciplinary; neurosurgical vault expansion in infancy; Le Fort III / monobloc advancement for midface; orbital decompression if corneal exposure
Congenital Orbital Tumors

Dermoid cyst: most common benign orbital tumor in children; ectodermal inclusion at suture lines; superotemporal (zygomaticofrontal suture, most common); deep orbital dermoids erode bone; excise intact (rupture = intense inflammation)
Teratoma: rare; all 3 germ layers; massive proptosis at birth; surgical excision
Ectopic lacrimal gland: superotemporal orbit; no ductal connection; slow proptosis; excision
Orbital Inflammatory & Infectious Disorders
Orbital Cellulitis
Preseptal (periorbital): anterior to orbital septum; lid swelling + erythema; no proptosis, no motility restriction; usually Staph/Strep; oral antibiotics; admit if young, systemic, no improvement

Orbital (postseptal): proptosis + restricted motility + pain on movement + chemosis + fever; most from ethmoidal sinusitis; Staph aureus, Strep, H. influenzae; risk: cavernous sinus thrombosis, ON compression, vision loss
Chandler classification: I (inflammatory oedema) → II (orbital oedema) → III (subperiosteal abscess) → IV (orbital abscess) → V (cavernous sinus thrombosis)
Management: IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone + vancomycin); CT scan; surgical drainage for: SPA not responding 24-48h, large abscess, vision threat, frontal sinusitis, all type IV/V
Cavernous sinus thrombosis: bilateral signs, meningism, high fever, septic emboli; IV heparin (controversial) + broad antibiotics
Life-Threatening Infections

Mucormycosis: Rhizopus/Mucor; diabetic ketoacidosis or immunocompromised (HIV, haematologic malignancy); rapidly fatal; black eschar nasal mucosa; angioinvasive → cavernous sinus; orbital apex syndrome; treatment: liposomal amphotericin B + aggressive surgical debridement ± exenteration; high mortality
Necrotising fasciitis: rare periorbital; polymicrobial; rapidly progressive; systemic toxicity; crepitus (gas on CT); immediate surgical debridement + IV antibiotics; high mortality
Aspergillosis: chronic invasive (immunocompetent) vs acute invasive (immunocompromised); CT: hyperdense mass in sinus; treat with voriconazole + surgical debridement
Thyroid Eye Disease (TED/Graves Ophthalmopathy)
Pathophysiology
Autoimmune: TSH-R Ab activates orbital fibroblasts → glycosaminoglycan accumulation + adipogenesis → EOM + fat expansion → proptosis
IGF-1R (insulin-like growth factor 1 receptor) also implicated; target for teprotumumab
Clinical Features
Lid retraction (most common sign; Dalrymple sign) + lid lag (von Graefe sign); proptosis; periorbital oedema; chemosis
Restrictive myopathy: IR most common → limited upgaze; then MR → limited abduction; SO + LR least affected
Dysthyroid optic neuropathy (DON): apical crowding → compressive optic neuropathy; RAPD; colour desaturation; urgent treatment
Assessment Tools
CAS (Clinical Activity Score): 7-point scale; ≥3/7 = active disease; assess: pain at rest, pain on movement, redness of lid, redness of conjunctiva, chemosis, swelling of caruncle, lid oedema
NOSPECS classification: severity grading (No signs, Only signs, Soft tissue, Proptosis, EOM, Cornea, Sight)
Hertel exophthalmometer: proptosis measurement; normal <20mm; >21mm or asymmetry >2mm = significant
Management
Thyroid control: euthyroid state; avoid radioiodine in active moderate-severe TED (worsens)
Smoking cessation: strongly worsens TED; doubles risk; must advise

Active disease (CAS ≥3): IV methylprednisolone pulse (European standard; 500mg weekly ×6 then 250mg ×6); teprotumumab (anti-IGF-1R; FDA approved; reduces proptosis ~2-3mm); selenium supplementation for mild disease
DON: IV steroids immediately; orbital decompression if no improvement within 2 weeks
Surgical sequence (inactive disease only): 1. Orbital decompression (if needed) → 2. Strabismus surgery (adjustable sutures) → 3. Eyelid surgery (lid retraction/ptosis)

Orbital decompression: remove fat ± bony walls; 1-wall (lateral, safest; ~3mm), 2-wall (medial+floor, most common; ~4-5mm), 3-wall (lateral+medial+floor; up to 6mm); balanced decompression (medial+lateral) reduces induced strabismus
Non-Infectious Orbital Inflammation
Nonspecific Orbital Inflammation (NSOI / Orbital Pseudotumor)
Most common cause of painful orbital mass in adults; no identifiable aetiology; idiopathic inflammatory; any orbital structure
Subtypes: myositic (EOM, including tendon = distinguishes from TED), dacryoadenitis (lacrimal gland), sclerosing (fibrous, least responsive to steroids), diffuse, Tolosa-Hunt (cavernous sinus)
Diagnosis of exclusion: r/o lymphoma (biopsy!), TED, IgG4-RD, sarcoid, vasculitis, infection
Treatment: oral prednisolone 1mg/kg/day; dramatic response (within 48-72h) is characteristic and diagnostic; if no response → biopsy urgently (may be lymphoma)

IgG4-Related Disease (IgG4-RD): systemic fibro-inflammatory; elevated serum IgG4; orbital + lacrimal gland involvement; bilateral; sclerosing lesions; biopsy essential (storiform fibrosis, obliterative phlebitis, IgG4:IgG ratio >40%); treat with steroids ± rituximab
Sarcoidosis: granulomatous; lacrimal gland most common orbital site; bilateral parotid + lacrimal enlargement (Heerfordt syndrome); CN VII palsy; serum ACE, chest CT; treat with systemic steroids
GPA (Granulomatosis with polyangiitis/Wegener): c-ANCA; orbital mass (sclerosing NSOI-like); saddle-nose deformity; renal/pulmonary; treat with rituximab or cyclophosphamide + steroids
Orbital Neoplasms and Malformations
Vascular Lesions

Infantile (capillary) hemangioma: most common orbital tumor in children; rapid growth (6-18 months) then involutes; proptosis + amblyopia risk; propranolol oral (first-line); intralesional triamcinolone if unresponsive

Lymphatic malformation: non-involuting; infiltrative; worsens with URTI (lymphoid hyperplasia); chocolate cyst (spontaneous haemorrhage); treat acutely for compressive symptoms; sclerotherapy (doxycycline, OK-432) or surgical debulking
Distensible venous malformation (orbital varix): dilates with Valsalva/head-down; intermittent proptosis; CT in Valsalva position; sclerotherapy or surgical excision

Cavernous venous malformation (cavernous hemangioma): most common benign orbital tumor in adults; well-circumscribed; intraconal; slow painless proptosis; high internal reflectivity on A-scan; no spontaneous regression; surgical excision if symptomatic
Carotid-Cavernous Fistula (CCF)
Direct (high-flow): ICA → CS; usually traumatic; pulsatile proptosis + bruit + chemosis + elevated IOP + dilated arterialized episcleral vessels + CN palsies
Indirect/dural (low-flow): dural branches → CS; spontaneous; older women; subtle; red eye + raised IOP
Diagnosis: CT/MRI shows dilated superior ophthalmic vein; DSA (gold standard) confirms and allows treatment
Treatment: endovascular embolisation (transarterial coil; direct CCF) or transvenous (via inferior petrosal sinus or SOV); direct compression of carotid can help indirect CCF
Neural Tumors
Optic Nerve Glioma
Pilocytic astrocytoma; most in children <10 years; 50% associated with NF1
NF1-associated: usually bilateral; indolent; observe; rarely treated
Sporadic: more aggressive; may involve chiasm/hypothalamus; vision loss; proptosis
MRI: fusiform thickening of ON; kinked appearance; no kinking = dolichoectasia (tortuous)
Treatment: controversial; observe if stable/NF1; chemotherapy (carboplatin+vincristine) if progressive; radiation if severe; surgery only if blind eye and disfiguring proptosis

Optic nerve sheath meningioma: adult women; ON compression; optociliary shunt vessels (chronic compression); CT: tram-track calcification; MRI fat-sat: enhancement around ON; treatment: fractionated stereotactic radiotherapy (FSRT); surgery sacrifices vision
Neurofibroma: NF1; plexiform = pathognomonic (S-shaped lid, pulsatile proptosis from absent greater wing of sphenoid); diffuse + solitary types; surgery if disfiguring; high recurrence
Schwannoma: encapsulated; usually superior orbit; well-defined; surgical excision
Mesenchymal Tumors

Rhabdomyosarcoma: most common PRIMARY malignant orbital tumor in children; mean age 7-8 years; rapidly progressive proptosis; superior nasal orbit most common; CT: poorly defined soft tissue ± bone erosion; biopsy then multimodal (chemotherapy + radiation); 90% survival
Fibrous histiocytoma (solitary fibrous tumor): adults; well-encapsulated; surgical excision; low malignant potential
Lymphoproliferative Disorders

Orbital lymphoma: most common primary orbital malignancy in adults; usually MALT (marginal zone B-cell); salmon-colored conjunctival/forniceal lesion ('salmon patch'); painless; bilateral in 25%; 20-30% systemic lymphoma
Staging: PET-CT + bone marrow biopsy; treatment: radiation (local MALT) or systemic chemo (high-grade/bilateral/systemic)
Distinction from pseudotumor: lymphoma = no pain, no dramatic steroid response; biopsy essential
Lacrimal Gland Tumors
Epithelial tumors: 50% of lacrimal gland masses (other 50% = lymphoid/inflammatory)

Pleomorphic adenoma (benign mixed cell tumor): most common epithelial; painless, slow, superotemporal; CT: smooth bony fossa (remodelling, no erosion); EXCISE INTACT (capsule violation → malignant transformation/recurrence); do NOT biopsy first

Adenoid cystic carcinoma (ACC): most common malignant; PAINFUL (perineural invasion); rapid; bone erosion on CT; cylindromatous (cribriform) pattern with Swiss-cheese appearance; aggressive surgery + radiation; 5-year survival ~20-50%; perineural spread to intracranial
Rule of 50s: 50% LG masses = epithelial; 50% epithelial = benign; 50% benign = pleomorphic adenoma
Orbital Metastases
Adults: breast (most common; can cause enophthalmos = scirrhous type), lung (2nd), prostate, renal cell, GI; any age: neuroblastoma (raccoon eyes in children)
Presentation: rapid proptosis, motility restriction, pain; enophthalmos (breast scirrhous type)
Management: palliation; systemic treatment; radiation for pain/vision; biopsy if no known primary
Orbital Trauma
Orbital Floor Fractures (Blow-Out)
Mechanism: blunt trauma to globe/orbit → increased intraorbital pressure → floor fractures at infraorbital groove (thinnest)
Signs: enophthalmos (fat/muscle herniation into maxillary sinus), hypoglobus, diplopia (inferior/upgaze restriction from IR/IO entrapment), infraorbital hypoaesthesia (V2)
CT: coronal and axial; shows fracture defect, herniation of orbital contents, 'trapdoor' fracture (children = hinge; EOM entrapment)

Indications for surgery: enophthalmos >2mm, restricted motility with strabismus (forced duction positive), trapdoor with muscle entrapment (URGENT in children; oculocardiac reflex, ischaemic muscle), large defect >50% floor
Timing: early (within 2 weeks) if indicated; delay for oedema resolution (7-10 days) unless trapdoor with entrapment (children = same day)
Surgical approach: subciliary, transconjunctival, or transantral; implant (titanium mesh, porous polyethylene, nylon foil) to cover defect
Other Orbital Fractures
Zygomatic (tripod) fracture: zygomatic arch + ZF suture + ZM suture; malar flattening; trismus; lateral canthal dystopia; orbital floor involvement; ORIF via multiple approaches
Orbital roof fracture: pneumocephalus; pulsatile proptosis (dural tear → CSF); enophthalmos; frontal lobe injury; neurosurgical consultation
Medial wall fracture: medial rectus entrapment → adduction restriction; nose blowing contraindicated (emphysema); often observed unless significant entrapment
Le Fort fractures: midfacial; I = floating palate; II = pyramidal; III = craniofacial dysjunction; all involve pterygoid plates; panfacial fractures
Orbital apex fracture: ON injury + CN palsy; optic canal decompression if significant vision loss
Orbital Compartment Syndrome (OCS)
OPHTHALMIC EMERGENCY; retrobulbar haemorrhage → elevated intraorbital pressure → ON ischaemia → irreversible blindness within 90-120 minutes
Signs: proptosis + rock-hard orbit + loss of RAPD + vision loss + markedly elevated IOP; severe pain

Treatment: IMMEDIATE lateral canthotomy + inferior cantholysis at bedside (do NOT wait for CT or theatre); releases orbital septum; reduces orbital pressure; then inferior cantholysis if needed; then CT and surgery
Also: IV mannitol, acetazolamide, high-flow O₂ adjuncts; definitive treatment is surgical drainage of haematoma
Traumatic optic neuropathy: indirect injury (forces transmitted to optic canal); no proven treatment; high-dose steroids controversial (IONTS showed no benefit over observation)
Intraorbital foreign body: CT orbits (not MRI if metallic); organic (wood) can mimic fat on CT; remove if causing symptoms, infection, or in contact with globe; inert metal can often observe
Orbital Surgery
Surgical Approaches
Superior approach: eyelid crease incision; upper eyelid; access to superior orbit, LG, superior rectus; risk: ptosis, LPS injury
Inferior approach: transconjunctival (inside lower fornix) or subciliary; floor, inferior orbit; risk: ectropion, lower lid retraction
Medial approach: Lynch incision (skin between medial canthus and nose) or transcaruncular (through caruncle); medial orbit, ethmoid; risk: DCR injury, epiphora
Lateral approach: lateral canthotomy + cantholysis; Kronlein approach (removes lateral wall); deep lateral intraconal access; lacrimal gland, lateral rectus; risk: lateral canthal dystopia
Orbital Decompression
Indications: DON (urgent), disfiguring proptosis, corneal exposure, pain, headache due to orbital pressure (TED, IIH-related)
Approaches: endoscopic (medial/medial+floor, most common for TED; ENT collaboration), lateral (fat ± bone), balanced (medial+lateral, fewer strabismus complications), 3-wall
Complications: CSF leak (roof), infraorbital hypoaesthesia (floor), new strabismus (medial wall — displaces IR medially), enophthalmos (overcorrection), haemorrhage, worsened ptosis
Complications of Orbital Surgery
Retrobulbar haemorrhage: most feared; lateral canthotomy + inferior cantholysis immediately
Infection: orbital cellulitis post-operatively
Strabismus: new or worsened; most common after medial wall decompression
Enophthalmos: over-decompression; may need re-implantation
CSF leak: roof surgery; persistent drainage → urgent neurosurgical consultation
The Anophthalmic Socket
Enucleation vs Evisceration vs Exenteration
Evisceration: removes intraocular contents; preserves scleral shell; better motility; risk of sympathetic ophthalmia (remove all uveal tissue); contraindicated if intraocular malignancy
Enucleation: removes entire globe; indicated for intraocular tumour (retinoblastoma, uveal melanoma), blind painful eye, severe trauma, infection; long optic nerve stump needed for Rb staging

Exenteration: removes all orbital contents (globe + lids + orbital fat ± bony walls); for malignancy (sebaceous carcinoma, invasive SCC, invasive melanoma, mucormycosis); lid-sparing if possible for prosthetic fitting
Orbital Implants
Placed at enucleation/evisceration to restore volume and provide motility
Porous polyethylene (Medpor): allows fibrovascular ingrowth; pegged implants for direct prosthesis coupling; good motility
Hydroxyapatite: porous coralline calcium phosphate; biological; fibrovascular ingrowth; can be pegged
Acrylic/PMMA: non-porous; simple; less motility; no pegging
Size: 20-22mm diameter to fill volume; fat graft adjunct for additional volume
Socket Complications
Implant exposure/extrusion: most common complication; thin conjunctiva over implant; treat with patch graft (buccal mucosa, amniotic membrane) or scleral patch; if extrusion → remove implant
Deep superior sulcus: inadequate volume; add subconjunctival fat graft or larger prosthesis
Contracted socket: cicatricial process; progressive fornix shortening; treat with spacer grafts (buccal mucosa, dermis fat graft)
Ptosis/ectropion/entropion: mechanical or due to poor socket volume; lid surgery after optimising socket volume
Prosthesis: custom acrylic; painted to match fellow eye; polished every 6-12 months; re-made every 5 years; motility from implant + socket tissue transmitted to prosthesis
PART II: PERIOCULAR SOFT TISSUES
Facial & Eyelid Anatomy
Facial Layers
SMAS (superficial musculoaponeurotic system): fibromuscular layer deep to subcutaneous fat; connects mimetic muscles; continuations: galea (forehead), platysma (neck), temporoparietal fascia (temple)
Facial danger zones: superficial temporal region (temporal branch of CN VII), nasolabial fold (buccal branches), marginal mandibular nerve (along mandible)
Fascial spaces: potential spaces between layers; surgical dissection planes
Mimetic Muscles
Orbicularis oculi: CN VII; pretarsal (blink + lacrimal pump) + preseptal (eyelid closure) + orbital (forced closure + brow depression)
Frontalis: CN VII; raises brow; no bony attachments; inserts into skin + procerus + corrugators
Corrugators: glabellar frown lines; depresses medial brow; CN VII
Procerus: CN VII; transverse nasal root lines (bunny lines)
Facial Nerve (CN VII)

Exits stylomastoid foramen → parotid gland → 5 terminal branches: Temporal (forehead, upper lid), Zygomatic (lateral canthus, lower lid), Buccal (midface, orbicularis), Marginal mandibular (lower lip depressors), Cervical (platysma)
Mnemonic: To Zanzibar By Motor Car
Temporal branch: crosses zygomatic arch in superficial temporal fascia; most vulnerable in temporal dissection (facelift)
Marginal mandibular: runs below mandible; vulnerable in neck dissection + parotid surgery; injury → lower lip asymmetry
Eyelid Layers
Anterior lamella: skin (thinnest in body, 0.5-1mm) + orbicularis oculi
Middle lamella: orbital septum (from arcus marginalis to levator aponeurosis [upper] or capsulopalpebral fascia [lower])
Posterior lamella: tarsus (dense fibrous; upper = 8-10mm, lower = 4-5mm) + conjunctiva
Critical principle: in reconstruction, replace like with like; use at least 2 of 3 layers from different sources to avoid scarring
Upper Eyelid Retractors
Levator palpebrae superioris: CN III; origin lesser wing sphenoid (above annulus of Zinn); 37-40mm long; aponeurosis inserts into upper third of tarsus + skin (creates lid crease)
Müller (superior tarsal) muscle: sympathetic; inserts superior tarsus; 10-12mm; contributes 2mm of lid elevation; Horner syndrome: 2mm ptosis from Müller dysfunction
Whitnall ligament: condensation of levator sheath 10-12mm above tarsus; fulcrum changes direction of levator force from posterior-anterior to superior-inferior
Canthal Tendons
Medial canthal tendon (MCT): anterior limb (to anterior lacrimal crest) + posterior limb (to posterior lacrimal crest) + superior limb (to frontal bone)
Posterior limb of MCT: most important for lid apposition to globe; must be reattached in medial canthal reconstruction
Lateral canthal tendon (LCT): inserts into Whitnall tubercle (2-3mm inside lateral orbital rim); weaker than MCT; important in lid tightening procedures
Eyelid Disorders and Neoplasms
Congenital Eyelid Anomalies

BPES (Blepharophimosis-Ptosis-Epicanthus Inversus Syndrome): AD (FOXL2); bilateral ptosis + blepharophimosis (horizontal fissure shortening) + epicanthus inversus + telecanthus; type I (with premature ovarian failure) + type II (without); surgery: epicanthus correction + ptosis repair + lateral canthoplasty

Congenital ptosis: poor levator function (LF <4mm); levator dysgenesis (fibrous replacement); lid lag on downgaze; treat urgently if amblyopia risk; frontalis sling if LF <4mm; levator resection if LF 4-7mm
Epiblepharon: horizontal skin fold rides over lid margin; lashes rotated inward; common Asian children; spontaneous resolution; surgical correction if corneal erosion

Epicanthus: skin fold from upper to lower lid at medial canthus; types: inversus (most common), tarsalis, palpebralis, supraciliaris; corrected with Y-V or Mustardé procedure if functional/cosmetic issue
Acquired Eyelid Disorders

Chalazion: chronic sterile lipogranulomatous inflammation of Meibomian gland; firm non-tender nodule; warm compresses → steroid injection (triamcinolone 0.1-0.2mL) → incision and curettage (I&C) from posterior approach; recurrent = biopsy (r/o sebaceous carcinoma)
Hordeolum: acute Staphylococcal infection; external (Zeis/Moll glands, at lash base) vs internal (Meibomian gland); warm compresses; topical antibiotics; I&C if pointing
Floppy eyelid syndrome: rubbery upper tarsus; spontaneous eversion overnight; papillary conjunctivitis; obese middle-aged men; associated OSA (70%); CPAP + lid taping; pentagonal wedge resection
Benign Eyelid Lesions
Squamous papilloma: HPV or unrelated; filiform or sessile; excision ± base cautery
Seborrhoeic keratosis: verrucous stuck-on; greasy brown; shave excision or cryotherapy
Keratoacanthoma: rapidly growing; volcano crater; may spontaneously regress; excise to confirm (resembles SCC)
Xanthelasma: yellow plaques medial eyelids; lipid-laden histiocytes; check lipids; not always associated with hyperlipidaemia; treat with TCA peel, CO₂ laser, surgical excision; high recurrence rate
Syringoma: eccrine sweat duct adenoma; small flesh-coloured papules; lower eyelids; women; multiple; CO₂ laser ablation
Sebaceous hyperplasia: enlarged sebaceous glands; donut-shaped; telangiectasia; biopsy vs sebaceous carcinoma
Pilomatricoma: calcifying epithelioma (hair matrix cells); children; hard; tent sign; excision
Naevus: melanocytic; junctional, compound, intradermal types; intradermal most common eyelid naevus; observe or excise
Capillary hemangioma: see orbital section; strawberry; propranolol
Malignant Eyelid Tumors
Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC)
Most common malignant eyelid tumor (90%); lower lid > medial canthus > upper lid > lateral canthus
Subtypes: nodular (most common; pearly, telangiectatic), morphoeaform/sclerosing (most aggressive; scar-like; ill-defined; higher recurrence), superficial, pigmented
Treatment: Mohs micrographic surgery (gold standard; margin-controlled); wide local excision with frozen sections; complete excision essential; radiation for non-surgical candidates
Rarely metastasizes (<0.1%); recurrence risk: morphoeaform, medial canthus, large tumors
Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC)
Second most common; upper lid and medial canthus; sun exposure, radiation, HPV, xeroderma pigmentosum
Keratinizing; ulcerating; indurated; perineural invasion; metastases possible (5-10%)
Treatment: wide excision with margin control; sentinel lymph node biopsy if large/perineural; adjuvant radiation for +margins/perineural invasion
Sebaceous Carcinoma
Arises from Meibomian glands or Zeis glands; upper lid most common (more Meibomian glands)
Great masquerader: mimics chronic chalazion, blepharitis, unilateral papillary conjunctivitis
Pagetoid spread: intraepithelial spread to conjunctiva/cornea; map biopsies essential
High mortality (14-25%); regional lymph node mets; radical wide excision + map biopsies + sentinel LN biopsy; consider exenteration if pagetoid spread extensive
Melanoma
Rare (<1% eyelid malignancies); nodular or superficial spreading
Lentigo maligna melanoma: common periocular; arises from lentigo maligna; slow-growing; treat with wide excision; Mohs or staged excision
Wide excision + sentinel LN biopsy; systemic staging
Merkel cell carcinoma: rare; aggressive neuroendocrine carcinoma; elderly immunocompromised; violaceous nodule; wide excision + sentinel LN biopsy + radiation; high recurrence/mets
Reconstructive Eyelid Surgery
Eyelid Trauma
Principles: clean wound, copious irrigation, close in layers, preserve tissue, realign grey line and lash line precisely

Canalicular laceration: medial lid laceration (especially if dog bite); assume canalicular involvement until proven otherwise; repair with silicone tube (monocanalicular stent) under operating microscope
Dog bites: high infection risk (Pasteurella, Capnocytophaga); irrigate copiously; primary closure (face heals well); IV amoxicillin-clavulanate; rabies prophylaxis if needed
Burns: thermal → contracture → cicatricial ectropion; initial lubrication and wound care; delayed reconstruction after maturation (6-12 months)
Eyelid Reconstruction Principles
Goal: restore functional anatomy and cosmesis; avoid corneal exposure; re-create posterior and anterior lamellae
<25% defect: primary closure; advance tissue with canthotomy/cantholysis
25-50% defect: lateral cantholysis + advancement
50-75% defect: Tenzel semicircular flap (lateral advancement flap for upper or lower lid)
>50% lower lid: Hughes tarsoconjunctival flap (posterior lamella from upper lid tarsus) + skin graft for anterior lamella; bilamellar; second stage at 4-6 weeks to open
>50% upper lid: Cutler-Beard flap (full-thickness lower lid advancement through lower eyelid) + tarsus graft; bilamellar; second stage at 6-8 weeks
Medial canthal reconstruction: need to reconstruct posterior MCT; Mustardé flap (large cheek rotation flap); paramedian forehead flap for large defects; forehead flap for nasal defects
Periocular Malpositions and Involutional Changes
Ectropion (lid turns outward)
Involutional (most common): horizontal lid laxity + lower lid retractor weakness; medial and/or lateral; medial punctal eversion → epiphora
Surgery: lateral tarsal strip (LTS) for horizontal laxity; medial spindle excision for medial ectropion; retractor advancement for lower lid
Paralytic: CN VII palsy → orbicularis weakness → lid falls; weight tape + lubrication; gold weight implant (upper lid); lateral tarsorrhaphy; LTS
Cicatricial: anterior lamellar shortening (scar, actinic damage, SJS); release scar + skin graft (full-thickness skin graft, usually from upper lid or retroauricular)
Mechanical: lesion/mass pulling lid outward; treat underlying cause
Entropion (lid turns inward)
Involutional (most common): horizontal lid laxity + lower lid retractor disinsertion + overriding preseptal orbicularis; lower lid; lashes abrade cornea
Quick fix: tape (temporary), Quickert sutures (outpatient; vertically placed sutures evert lid); definitive: LTS + retractor advancement + orbicularis tuck
Cicatricial: posterior lamellar shortening (trachoma, SJS, MMP); lashes turn in; mucous membrane graft (buccal or conjunctival) to lengthen posterior lamella; lamella splitting
Congenital: horizontal laxity; often resolves; surgery if corneal erosion
Trichiasis
Misdirected lashes touching globe; posterior cornea/conjunctiva
Epilation (temporary); electrolysis (single lashes); cryotherapy (lid margin; most effective for multiple lashes; risk: depigmentation, lid notching); argon laser ablation; lash bulb excision
Blepharoptosis (Ptosis)
Evaluation
MRD1 (margin reflex distance): distance from corneal light reflex to upper lid margin; normal 4-5mm; MRD1 <2mm = significant ptosis
Levator function (LF): excursion from full downgaze to full upgaze while fixing brow; normal >12mm; good = 8-12mm; fair = 5-7mm; poor = <4mm
Rule out: Horner (phenylephrine test lifts lid — Müller muscle dependent), MG (ice test, fatigue), CN III palsy, jaw-winking (Marcus Gunn)
Phenylephrine test: 1 drop 2.5% → if lid rises >1.5mm → Müller muscle-dependent → Müller muscle conjunctival resection (MMCR/Fasanella-Servat) will work
Classification
Aponeurotic (involutional): most common acquired; high lid crease; normal LF; aponeurosis disinsertion; treat with levator aponeurosis advancement
Myogenic: CPEO (bilateral; mitochondrial; see MG for diagnosis); myotonic dystrophy (facial diplegia); MG (fatigable; variable)
Neurogenic: CN III palsy (+ ophthalmoplegia + mydriasis if compressive); Horner (2mm ptosis; miosis; anhidrosis)
Mechanical: lid mass, oedema, scarring
Congenital: poor levator function; fibrous replacement
Management
LF >8mm: levator aponeurosis advancement (external approach; most common ptosis procedure)
LF 5-7mm (fair): maximal levator resection
LF <4mm (poor): frontalis sling (silicone rod or autologous fascia lata from thigh)
Phenylephrine test positive: MMCR (Müller muscle conjunctival resection / Fasanella-Servat); posterior approach; amount of tissue resected = mm determined by phenylephrine response
Children: treat urgently if visual axis obstructed (deprivation amblyopia) or abnormal head posture; frontalis sling usually required
Eyelid Retraction
Most common cause: thyroid eye disease (Dalrymple sign); others: contralateral ptosis (Hering's law), dorsal midbrain syndrome (Collier sign), high myopia, surgical overcorrection
Treatment: treat underlying cause; TED-related: recession of levator aponeurosis (posterior approach) + Müller muscle recession; injectable hyaluronic acid (temporary, office procedure)
Facial Paralysis (CN VII)
Lagophthalmos: inability to close eye → corneal exposure; Bell's phenomenon (upward eye roll) protective
Corneal protection: lubricants (q1h day + ointment night), moisture chamber, scleral lens, taping/patching
Short-term/reversible: gold weight implant (upper lid; 1.0-1.6g; gravity assists closure); spring implant; botulinum toxin to lower lid (lifts lower lid)
Long-term/permanent: platinum chain implant; lower lid elevation (LTS + spacer graft); medial canthoplasty; brow elevation (direct or endoscopic)
Facial Dystonia

Benign essential blepharospasm (BEB): involuntary bilateral orbicularis spasm; functional blindness; middle-aged women; distinguish from hemifacial spasm (unilateral) and apraxia of lid opening (agonist failure not orbicularis spasm)
Treatment: botulinum toxin (OnabotulinumtoxinA/Botox; AbobotulinumtoxinA/Dysport) injections to orbicularis; repeat every 3-4 months; myectomy for severe cases unresponsive to BoNT
Hemifacial spasm: unilateral; CN VII compression at root entry zone; treat with BoNT or MVD (microvascular decompression)
Involutional Periorbital Changes

Dermatochalasis: excess upper eyelid skin ± herniated orbital fat; causes visual field loss (superior field); functional blepharoplasty covered by insurance if field impaired; cosmetic if asymptomatic
Blepharochalasis: episodic lid oedema → skin laxity → ptosis; young adults; rare; repair between attacks

Brow ptosis: frontalis compensation for ptosis; low medial brow (CN VII innervates lateral frontalis more) + lateral brow ptosis; assess brow position before upper blepharoplasty; fix brow first or simultaneous
Blepharoplasty
Upper lid: skin mark with lid open and closed; excise skin ± muscle ± fat; preserve >20mm skin above lashes to lid crease; do not remove too much fat (causes deep superior sulcus)
Lower lid approaches: skin/skin-muscle flap (anterior) or transconjunctival (no skin scar; fat only); fat excision vs repositioning (repositioning preferred in Asian anatomy, prevents hollowed look)
Complications: lagophthalmos/exposure (over-resection of skin), ectropion, dry eye, diplopia (fat haemostasis trauma to IR), retrobulbar haemorrhage (rare, vision-threatening), asymmetry
Asian blepharoplasty: create or enhance lid crease (absent or low in monolid); suture or incisional technique
Facial Rejuvenation
Pathogenesis of Facial Ageing
Gravitational descent: skin + SMAS laxity; ptosis of fat compartments
Volume deflation: fat atrophy, bone resorption (orbital rim expansion, maxillary retrusion), muscle atrophy
Skin changes: UV damage, collagen/elastin degradation, actinic keratoses; thinning epidermis
Dynamic rhytids: wrinkles from repeated mimetic muscle contraction (glabellar, crow's feet, forehead)
Non-Surgical Rejuvenation
Chemical peels: superficial (glycolic acid, TCA 10-30%: epidermis); medium (TCA 35%, Jessner+TCA); deep (phenol: dermis; profound rejuvenation but prolonged healing, hypopigmentation risk)

Laser resurfacing: ablative (CO₂ 10,600nm; Er:YAG 2940nm; full dermis; significant downtime; best results); non-ablative (Nd:YAG, diode; less downtime, multiple sessions); fractional (ablates columns leaving bridges for rapid re-epithelialization)

Botulinum toxin A (cosmetic): temporarily weakens mimetic muscles; glabellar lines (#1 indication; corrugators + procerus); forehead (frontalis); crow's feet (lateral orbicularis); brow shaping; hyperhidrosis; platysmal bands; onset 3-5 days, peak 2 weeks, duration 3-4 months

Soft tissue fillers: hyaluronic acid (Juvederm, Restylane; reversible with hyaluronidase; most commonly used); calcium hydroxyapatite (Radiesse; collagen stimulator); PLLA (Sculptra; gradual volumisation); autologous fat (permanent but unpredictable resorption)

Filler vascular complications: blindness from inadvertent intra-arterial injection (glabella, nasolabial fold); immediate hyaluronidase for HA fillers; high-flow O₂; ocular massage; anterior chamber paracentesis; prevention: aspiration, low pressure, small boluses
Surgical Rejuvenation
Lower blepharoplasty: fat excision vs repositioning; skin-muscle flap vs transconjunctival; lateral canthal support essential; complications: ectropion, scleral show, dry eye

Forehead rejuvenation: direct brow lift (scar visible, for men); transpalpebral corrugator resection (through upper blepharoplasty incision); endoscopic brow lift (5 scalp ports; releases forehead tissue; internal fixation; minimal scar; most popular); coronal brow lift (scar behind hairline; severe brow ptosis)
Midface rejuvenation: malar fat pad descent → nasolabial fold; midface lift (endoscopic or open); fat grafting to restore volume
Lower face + neck: SMAS plication or SMASectomy; deep plane facelift (elevates SMAS as flap); platysma correction (platysmaplasty); good results but significant surgery
PART III: LACRIMAL SYSTEM
Lacrimal System: Development, Anatomy & Physiology
Secretory System Anatomy

Main lacrimal gland: orbital (larger) + palpebral lobes; separated by levator aponeurosis; superotemporal fornix; parasympathetic secretomotor (CN VII → greater petrosal → vidian → pterygopalatine ganglion → zygomatic → lacrimal)
Accessory glands: Krause (deep fornix) + Wolfring (above tarsal border); produce basal tear secretion
Tear film layers: lipid (Meibomian glands; prevents evaporation) + mucoaqueous (lacrimal + goblet cells)
Drainage System Anatomy
Puncta: 2 per lid; 6mm from medial canthus; upper punctum slightly lateral to lower
Canaliculi: 2mm vertical + 8mm horizontal; upper + lower join to form common canaliculus (90%); common canaliculus enters lacrimal sac at valve of Rosenmuller (valve against reflux)
Lacrimal sac: in lacrimal fossa (between anterior and posterior lacrimal crests); 12-15mm tall; nasolacrimal duct starts at fundus
Nasolacrimal duct: 12-15mm long in bone; opens into inferior meatus below inferior turbinate; valve of Hasner at lower end
Physiology
Lacrimal pump (Rosenmuller-Jones): blink → pretarsal orbicularis compresses ampullae → suction draws tears into canaliculi; relaxation → sac expands drawing tears in; requires lid apposition to globe
Tear production: 1-2μL/min basal; up to 30× reflex tearing; drainage: 80% via lacrimal system, 20% evaporation
Abnormalities of the Lacrimal System
Congenital Lacrimal Drainage Obstruction
Congenital NLD Obstruction (CNLDO)
Most common (6%); imperforate valve of Hasner; epiphora + discharge from birth; mucoid discharge (Staphylococcus); no conjunctival injection (distinguishes from conjunctivitis)
REGURGITATION test: pressure on lacrimal sac → mucoid material from punctum = sac-to-punctum patent; obstruction is at NLD level
90% resolve spontaneously within 12 months; lacrimal massage (Crigler technique: compress sac, stroke downward) 2-3×/day; topical antibiotics for acute episodes
Probing: age 12-13 months (if persists); office or GA; success >90%; silicone tube intubation if probing fails; nasolacrimal balloon dilation as alternative
DCR: if intubation fails or older child (>3-4 years)
Dacryocystocele (Dacryocele)
Distended lacrimal sac at birth; blue-grey medial canthal mass; from distal NLD obstruction + imperforate common canaliculus (valve acts as one-way valve)
Nasal cyst: often extends into inferior meatus; can cause respiratory distress (neonates are obligate nasal breathers)
Treatment: early massage + probing; endoscopic nasal cyst marsupialization if nasal cyst causing respiratory distress; aggressive management in first days
Acquired Lacrimal Drainage Obstruction
Evaluation of Epiphora
Dye disappearance test (DDT): fluorescein in conjunctival fornix; normal = disappears in 5 min; retained = delayed drainage
Jones 1 test: primary dye test; fluorescein in nose with applicator at 5 min → positive = drainage patent under physiological conditions

Jones 2 test: secondary dye test; irrigate with saline after Jones 1; fluorescein in nose → partial obstruction; clear fluid → complete functional obstruction; no fluid → complete anatomical obstruction
Irrigation/probing: hard stop (metal on bone = patent to sac) vs soft stop (obstruction at common canaliculus or canaliculus)
Dacryocystography (DCG): radiographic; delineates anatomy of obstruction
Lacrimal scintigraphy: functional; physiological assessment
Punctal Disorders
Punctal stenosis: involutional (most common), drug-induced (docetaxel, 5-FU, epinephrine drops); epiphora; treat with punctal dilation → punctoplasty (1-snip, 2-snip, 3-snip)
Ectropion of punctum: see involutional ectropion; medial lid laxity; punctum turns outward; medial spindle procedure
Canalicular Obstruction
Common causes: herpes simplex (canaliculitis from scarring), actinomyces (see below), trauma, drugs (docetaxel, 5-FU, eye drops, radiation)
Monocanalicular obstruction: silicone tube stenting; Jones tube if uncorrectable
Acquired NLD Obstruction (ANLDO)
Most common cause of epiphora in adults; primary acquired (idiopathic, mostly postmenopausal women; chronic inflammation/fibrosis) or secondary (trauma, tumour, systemic disease, iatrogenic)
Treatment: DCR (dacryocystorhinostomy)
DCR (Dacryocystorhinostomy)
Gold standard for NLD obstruction; creates direct anastomosis between lacrimal sac and nasal cavity (middle meatus)
External DCR: Lynch incision; osteotomy; anterior + posterior flaps anastomosed; silicone tube intubation; ~95% success

Endoscopic (endonasal) DCR: no skin incision; transnasal laser or powered osteotomy; similar success rates; preferred for revision, bleeding disorders, cosmetic concerns; requires ENT collaboration or training
Silicone tube: Crawford or Ritleng; removed at 2-3 months post-op
Therapeutic Closure (Punctal Plugs)
For dry eye disease: plugs canaliculus to retain tears on ocular surface
Temporary: collagen (dissolves in days-weeks); silicone (removable); intracanalicular (Oasis, EagleFlex)
Permanent: thermal punctal cautery or argon laser punctal occlusion
Lacrimal Infections
Dacryoadenitis
Inflammation of lacrimal gland; S-shaped ptosis (upper lid follows gland shape); superotemporal swelling; pre-auricular lymphadenopathy
Acute: usually viral (mumps, EBV, CMV, herpes); self-limited; treat symptoms
Chronic: sarcoidosis, TB, IgG4-RD, lymphoma; biopsy essential; treat underlying disease
Canaliculitis
Actinomyces israelii (most common; gram-positive anaerobic filamentous bacterium); sulphur granules from punctum; 'lumpy punctum'; pouting punctum
Chronic unilateral red eye; medial lid; often misdiagnosed as blepharitis/conjunctivitis
Treatment: canaliculotomy (incise canaliculus to express concretions) + penicillin VK 250mg QID or topical penicillin irrigation; curettage of canaliculus
Dacryocystitis
Infection of lacrimal sac; from NLD obstruction + stasis; erythema + swelling + tenderness below medial canthus (BELOW MCT = lacrimal sac; above MCT = ethmoidal)
Organisms: Staph aureus (acute), Strep pneumoniae, H. influenzae; chronic: Staph epidermidis, Actinomyces
Acute: IV/oral antibiotics; warm compresses; do NOT probe during acute infection; drain abscess if pointing; DCR when acute episode resolved
Chronic: recurrent; fibrosis; mucocoele; definitive DCR
Lacrimal Neoplasms

Lacrimal gland tumours: see orbital neoplasms (pleomorphic adenoma, adenoid cystic carcinoma); epithelial tumours require different approach from lymphoid/inflammatory (do not biopsy pleomorphic adenoma)

Lacrimal drainage system tumours: papilloma (most common benign), oncocytoma, transitional cell carcinoma (most common malignant); epiphora + bloody discharge; CT/MRI for staging; DCR with tumour excision or radical excision
Top 30 Exam Facts
Orbital floor = thinnest at infraorbital groove (overlies maxillary sinus); medial wall = thinnest overall (lamina papyracea)
SOF contents inside annulus of Zinn: CN III (superior + inferior divisions), CN VI, nasociliary nerve; outside: lacrimal, frontal, trochlear (CN IV), superior ophthalmic vein
Optic canal: CN II + ophthalmic artery; lesser wing of sphenoid
Orbital compartment syndrome: LATERAL CANTHOTOMY + INFERIOR CANTHOLYSIS immediately; do not wait for imaging
Mucormycosis: DKA/immunocompromised; black nasal eschar; angioinvasive; liposomal amphotericin B + aggressive debridement; very high mortality
TED surgical sequence: decompression → strabismus surgery → eyelid surgery (in that order, only when disease inactive)
CAS ≥3/7 = active TED; IV methylprednisolone pulse; teprotumumab for proptosis reduction
Cavernous venous malformation (cavernous hemangioma): most common benign orbital tumor ADULTS; high internal reflectivity A-scan; surgical excision if symptomatic
Infantile capillary hemangioma: most common orbital tumor CHILDREN; propranolol first-line
Rhabdomyosarcoma: most common PRIMARY malignant orbital tumor in children; biopsy + chemo + radiation; 90% survival
Orbital lymphoma: MALT most common; salmon patch conjunctiva; painless; 25% systemic; radiation for local disease
Pleomorphic adenoma: excise INTACT (no biopsy first); capsule violation → malignant transformation
Adenoid cystic carcinoma: most common MALIGNANT lacrimal gland tumor; PAINFUL; perineural invasion; poor prognosis
Rule of 50s for lacrimal gland: 50% epithelial; 50% of epithelial = benign; 50% of benign = pleomorphic adenoma
Blow-out fracture: trapdoor + muscle entrapment in children = URGENT same-day surgery (oculocardiac reflex + ischaemic muscle)
Orbital NSOI (pseudotumor): painful; dramatic steroid response in 48-72h; if no response → biopsy urgently (r/o lymphoma)
CCF: pulsatile proptosis + bruit + red eye + raised IOP + arterialized vessels; treat with endovascular embolisation
Enucleation (not evisceration) for intraocular malignancy; long ON section for retinoblastoma staging
BCC most common malignant eyelid tumor (90%); morphoeaform = most aggressive; lower lid > medial canthus > upper lid
Sebaceous carcinoma: masquerades as chalazion/blepharitis; upper lid most common; pagetoid spread; map biopsies essential; high mortality
Hughes flap: tarsoconjunctival from upper lid to reconstruct >50% lower lid defect; second stage 4-6 weeks
Levator function determines ptosis surgery: LF <4mm → frontalis sling; LF >8mm → levator advancement; phenylephrine test positive → MMCR
MRD1 normal: 4-5mm; <2mm = significant ptosis; levator function normal: >12mm
Involutional entropion: lateral tarsal strip + retractor advancement + orbicularis tuck; Quickert sutures for temporary fix
Paralytic ectropion (CN VII palsy): gold weight implant upper lid; lateral tarsorrhaphy; lubrication to protect cornea
Dacryocystitis: swelling BELOW medial canthal tendon; do NOT probe during acute infection; IV antibiotics + delayed DCR
Canaliculitis: Actinomyces; sulphur granules + pouting punctum; canaliculotomy + penicillin; often misdiagnosed as conjunctivitis
DCR success rate: external ~95%; endoscopic similar; creates sac-to-nasal cavity anastomosis at middle meatus
Botulinum toxin cosmetic: glabellar lines #1 indication; duration 3-4 months; filler vascular complication → hyaluronidase immediately for HA fillers
Orbital NF1 signs: Lisch nodules (iris), optic nerve glioma, plexiform neurofibroma (S-shaped lid), pulsatile proptosis (absent greater sphenoid wing), café-au-lait spots
